title: The wrapper that was missing
steps.1.text: The heading and the sentence go inside `<body>`, not `<head>`.
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of wrapping it. Bring the heading and the sentence back — the task is to WRAP them, not remove them.
hint2.empty: Put the two lines back, then place the three tags around them.
why.not-code: There is not a single closed tag. Either you wrote something that is not HTML, or a tag is open and never closed.
hint2.not-code: Go top to bottom, tag by tag. Every open tag is looking for its closing one.
hint3.not-code: Remember: `head` ends before `body` begins. They are siblings, not one inside the other. The correct shape is `<head></head><body></body>`.
why.no-html: There is no wrapper. Everything on the page lives inside one tag — it wraps both the head and the body.
why.no-head: There is no head. It is empty for now, which is exactly why it is easy to skip. But two lessons from here you will need it — the page name and the character set go there.
hint2.no-head: `<head>` is the first thing inside `<html>`. It is empty for now — just `<head></head>`.
why.no-body: There is no body. Everything a person sees lives inside it. Your heading and sentence belong there.
hint2.no-body: `<body>` comes AFTER `</head>`, not inside it. They are siblings.
why.lost-content: The content is gone. The task is to WRAP what you already have — not to delete it and write it again.
hint2.lost-content: Do not delete. Wrap. The heading and the sentence stay — just inside `<body>`.
why.content-in-head: The wrapper is there, but something is in the wrong place.
hint2.content-in-head: Look at what sits between `<head>` and `</head>`. That part is the invisible one — information ABOUT the page goes there, not the page itself.
hint3.content-in-head: The skeleton in the lesson has an empty `<head>` and an empty `<body>`. Look at it again. The heading and the sentence show up on screen — so where do they belong?
hint4.content-in-head: Cut the two lines with `h1` and `p` out of the head. Put them between `<body>` and `</body>`.
blocks.1.text: So far you wrote bare text with tags and it worked. The browser is forgiving — it swallows whatever you give it and tries to show it.
blocks.2.text: But a real page has a wrapper. And it is not a formality.
blocks.5.text: Notice the arrangement: `head` and `body` are siblings, not one inside the other. The head ends before the body begins — and both sit inside `<html>`.
blocks.6.items.0: `<html>` wraps everything — this is the document itself.
blocks.6.items.1: `<head>` is information ABOUT the page. It is not visible.
blocks.6.items.2: `<body>` is the page itself. Everything visible is here.
blocks.8.text: `<body>` is the page a person sees. Everything you want to appear on screen goes here. Headings, sentences, images — all of it.
blocks.9.text: `<head>` is the opposite: none of it is visible. It holds information ABOUT the page — what it is called, what language it is in, who wrote it. Like a label on a box: not part of the contents, but it says what is inside.
blocks.10.text: The rule is simple and has no exceptions: an element that shows something on screen has no business in the head. Put `<h1>` there and the browser will silently move it into the body — your code will work and still be wrong.
blocks.12.text: The split dates from 1995 and the reason is written into the first specification: a document is organised as a head and a body, much like a memo. The head is a small unordered collection of information about the document. The body is an ordered sequence of content of arbitrary length.
blocks.13.text: And that is where the point is: this lets a program work out certain properties of the document — the title, for instance — without reading all of it. That is why the head is at the front and why it is small. Google, Facebook and screen readers do exactly this today.
blocks.15.text: Take the page and wrap it properly: `<html>` on the outside, `<head>` empty for now, and everything visible inside `<body>`.
blocks.16.text: Here is the finished shape and what the browser shows:
blocks.19.text: Identical to the old result. Nothing changed on screen. That is exactly why it was easy to skip — and exactly why you should get used to writing it before the head starts filling up.
blocks.21.items.1: `<head>` is for information ABOUT the page and is not visible.
blocks.21.items.3: Head and body are siblings, not one inside the other.
blocks.21.items.4: The head is separate so a machine can read only it.
walkthrough.0.text: Nothing was deleted. The two old lines are untouched — just moved inward, into the body.
walkthrough.1.text: `<head>` stays empty and that is perfectly valid. Over the next three lessons the page name, the declaration and the character set move in — each with its own lesson.
walkthrough.2.text: The indentation is not required. The browser collapses spaces, so you could write all of this on one line. It gets written because twenty lessons from now this file will be a hundred lines and you will be hunting for where each part ends.
